  "rationale": {
    "R1.2": "Three internal contradictions bear directly on logical consistency. (1) Supervised-vs-unsupervised contradiction: the Data-collection section explicitly uses 12,400 human-labeled pairs as 'ground truth for both training and evaluation,' yet the Deployment-scope section claims ROUTERSCAN is 'fully unsupervised' and 'requires no annotated examples … and no ground-truth labels of any kind.' These are mutually exclusive; the system as described is supervised. (2) AUC-to-prevalence non-sequitur: the Results section concludes 'from this AUC … the rate of anomalous routing decisions … is approximately 18 percent.' AUC measures rank-order discrimination, not prevalence; the 18% figure derives from the annotation labels set in advance, not from the AUC value. The inferential arrow is logically invalid. (3) 'Calibrated prevalence estimator' claim: the paper promotes ROUTERSCAN as a calibrated prevalence estimator on the strength of the AUC result, but calibration (reliability of predicted probabilities) and AUC are distinct properties; a high AUC does not establish calibration, nor does it licence prevalence estimation. All three break the premise–conclusion chain and are load-bearing logical failures."

rationale.R1.2: Three internal contradictions bear directly on logical consistency. (1) Supervised-vs-unsupervised contradiction: the Data-collection section explicitly uses 12,400 human-labeled pairs as 'ground truth for both training and evaluation,' yet the Deployment-scope section claims ROUTERSCAN is 'fully unsupervised' and 'requires no annotated examples … and no ground-truth labels of any kind.' These are mutually exclusive; the system as described is supervised. (2) AUC-to-prevalence non-sequitur: the Results section concludes 'from this AUC … the rate of anomalous routing decisions … is approximately 18 percent.' AUC measures rank-order discrimination, not prevalence; the 18% figure derives from the annotation labels set in advance, not from the AUC value. The inferential arrow is logically invalid. (3) 'Calibrated prevalence estimator' claim: the paper promotes ROUTERSCAN as a calibrated prevalence estimator on the strength of the AUC result, but calibration (reliability of predicted probabilities) and AUC are distinct properties; a high AUC does not establish calibration, nor does it licence prevalence estimation. All three break the premise–conclusion chain and are load-bearing logical failures.
